title: The Macro Minute: Will the one big, ugly bill survive Senate scrutiny?

Howdy team 42. It's your skipper here at
Darius Dell to present our macro minute
for Wednesday, June 4th, 2025. As
always, we'll start with the executive
summary from today's leadoff morning
note. If you would like to review the
analysis supporting these conclusions
and to see if you need to make any
changes in your portfolio to remain on
the right side of market risk, you need
to be a client of 42 macro. So today's
key macro question is, will the one big
ugly bill survive Senate scrutiny? Our
answer, Elon Musk called out President
Trump's tax and spending bill, called it
a disgusting abomination as Republican
fiscal hawks like Senator Mike Lee and
Ran Paul echoed criticism. In response,
House Speaker Mike Johnson called must
stance very disappointing and the White
House defended the legislation as one
big beautiful bill. Senate Majority
Leader John Thun said failure is not an
option on raising the debt limit with
Treasury having previously forecast the
US would exhaust borrowing authority by
August or September. Thun also said uh
the House salt deal must change to move
forward. The size and scope of the salt
cap increase, debt limit increase,
Medicaid work requirements, and clean
energy tax credits are all key avenues
for performative congressional
brinksmanship that investors should fade
as the one big ugly bill act gets
legislated this summer. In our view,
there is a high probability that uh that
ABBA, the one big ugly bill survives of
the reconciliation process intact. there
is a reasonable probability uh it
actually gets bigger in terms of its
impact on growing the national debt. Uh
and in our risk management nomenclature
when we say high probability that means
75% or above and reasonable probability
is 50 to 75% moderate probability is uh
25 to 50% and low probability zero to
25%. In addition to Elon's outcry,
Tesla's uh uh Tesla Energy warned that
cutting Biden era uh EV and clean energy
tax credits would quote unquote threaten
America's energy independence and the
reliability of our grid. A message Musk
uh boosted on X amid rising Republican
opposition to the bill's climate
rollbacks. We've consistently signaled
that fiscal large s was partially
responsible for perpetuating the
K-shaped nature of the US economy and
Tesla's whining about the tax credit
refill reminds us that rich people who
run companies like Tesla, SpaceX,
Palanteer, etc. make multiples more
money annually from federal government
outlays and tax breaks than poor people.
Yes, the system is rigged, but until
Congress turns the fiscal spigot off,
the US economy is likely to remain
resilient due to the outsiz influence
that rich people on the top part of the
K have on economic activity. Reminder,
bullish paradigm C actually turns this
bigot up, not off. So, be aware of that.
Transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard
is always a wrap- up with a question
from our community. This one's regarding
taxes. Says, "Hi, Darius. I see that
KISS performs better than the S&P in
general. However, uh how does it perform
uh versus SB with long-term buy and hold
strategy as opposed to the constant
buying and selling of KISS when you add
in taxes? Uh so, good question. Uh so,
uh one thing I two things I'll correct.
So, uh KISS has not performed better
than the S&P. Uh we don't benchmark KISS
against the S&P. KISS demonstrabably
trounces the thing that we're
benchmarked against, which is the uh
6040 portfolio. And then we also
benchmark KISS to a naked long portfolio
featuring 60% stocks, 30% gold, and 10%
Bitcoin, which is what KISS is when it's
maxed out, but it's not always maxed
out. So, KISS will raise cash when the
risk management overlays tell it to
raise cash. And so um you know when you
benchmark KISS versus you know fully
invested at all times 60 3010 stocks
gold Bitcoin it has an upside capture
ratio of about 90% and a downside
capture ratio less than 50%. So
essentially you're getting 90% of the
return you would get being fully
invested at all times um in uh 60%
stocks 30% gold and 10% Bitcoin with
less than half of the downside. So if
you wanted to be snake it long, 60%
stocks, 30% gold, 10% Bitcoin at all
times, you would get 100% of the return
and 100% of the downtime. What KISS does
is chops off the left tail of the
distribution while while continuing to
expose you to the right tail of the
distribution with its 90% upside capture
ratio and below 50% uh downside capture
ratio. So, uh, that's what KISS, uh,
does in a nutshell is it gives you the
opportunity to remain invested when
you're supposed to be invested while
also allowing you to sleep comfortably
light night knowing that you're not
going to blow up your family's nest egg,
face volatility drag uh, and the hope
and andor despair of of having to hope
that your portfolio can recover from a
market crash. You know, I think um, you
know, uh, I think a lot of investors my
age, you know, have never lived through
a you know, sustained bare market. The
last sustained bare market we had ended
when I started my career back in early
2009. And so as someone who's been on
Wall Street for as long as I have, who
has many institutional contacts and
clients as I have and has been around
the world many times over, you know,
educating and and collaborating with
institutional investors, Daredell has
never actually lived through a durable
bare market. And so think about that.
I'm almost 40 years old.
So, think about all the people who are
younger than me that have never seen or
experienced a durable bare market,
especially not with enough money to
actually care about. And so, to me, I
think um I think we've all gotten very
used to uh you know, max draw down
recoveries that are very short in length
in duration. It used to be the case that
max draw down recoveries from these you
know, doable bare markets take years.
And if you're at or near retirement,
you're you don't have years to recover
uh from losing, you know, 20 to 30,
maybe even 40% of the money that you
spent your entire life accumulating.
Again, you spend your entire life saving
money. And if you suffer a crash, you're
going to lose 20 minimum 20% of the the
money you made throughout the course of
your entire career. You're going to lose
30% or more if if your portfolio goes
down by that. So that's what case is
designed to do is to create, you know,
put sophisticated institutional grade
risk management over no overlays, the
kind of risk management overlays that
you see being employed at many of the
top institutions across global Wall
Street. We're taking that and we're
bringing it to 42 macro to to to retail
investors around the world because guess
what? They deserve it too. Everyone
deserves the opportunity to have their
portfolio riskmanaged by sophisticated,
you know, institutional grade uh risk
management overlays. And so I'm just
really grateful uh that you know we have
thousands of clients around the world
that are um participating in this. Uh
and so uh before I answer the question
there's one other thing I will say with
respect to KISS. So uh it says the the
question asks uh uh the question says
opposed to the constant buying and
selling KISS. Uh that is a flatout
misnomer. There is no constant buying
and selling of Kiss. Kiss trades way
less frequently than the average retail
investor. There are only two trades per
month on average in KISS. That most
retail investors are out there trading
their accounts more than two to three
times per day. We're talking two to two
trades per month on average. So, KISS is
a low volatility, low turnover
investment strategy that is designed to
expose retail investors to asset markets
in a way that helps them retire on time
and comfortably while chopping off the
left tail of the distribution that helps
them sleep well at night throughout that
entire process. That's it. And so, if
that sounds good to you, then obviously
come check out KISS. But, u getting back
into this question in taxes because to
me, I think this is a very important
question to answer. Uh when I when we
talk about taxes, uh first thing I would
ask is uh whenever someone asks me about
taxes is whose taxes are you talking
about? Not only do we have our own
individual tax situations as people, you
know, in terms of some people are
trading uh in a tax advantage account,
some people are trading in a taxable
brokerage account. You know, there's
different, you know, we have our own tax
situations as people. But guess what? 42
macro specifically has clients in 80
country, 80 plus countries around the
world. I think a little more over half.
Let's say somewhere 50 to 55% of our
client base is international. So you're
talking about 80 plus tax jurisdictions,
you know, with people with different tax
situations in each of those
jurisdictions. I mean, you're talking
about like a near infinite number of
potential tax, you know, combinations.
We can't build an investment strategy to
satisfy all those different tax
combinations. But what we can do is give
them access to sophisticated risk
management overlays that help them
eliminate volatility track from their
portfolio. It's up to them how they
choose to implement it. I think the
smart people who are following our KISS
process, well, everyone following our
KISS process is, in my opinion, smarter
than the average retail investor because
they figured out that this stuff is
better than what they've been doing.
Those are the smart people. But the
smartest of the smart people are
obviously transacting in in in tax
advantage accounts. Um, obviously, so
that that that's one way in which you
can um get around uh the fact that, you
know, riskmanaging financial markets
sometimes requires you to have taxable
gains andor um um um um you know, tax
losses. Um, so just be aware of that.
Um, and by the way, you you can't get
tax losses. You know, you don't get the
benefit of tax losses if you are a
long-term buy and hold. So, also be
aware of that. Those things probably
wash out over the course of time. Uh,
and the thing I'll say is um, another
thing on taxes is, you know, your
question about how you should be
treating exposing yourself to financial
markets is really um, it's really
incumbent upon who you are and where you
are in your investment journey. If
you're a young person, say younger than
me, you have decades to recover from a
crash in your portfolio. And so buy and
hold, it makes a lot more sense for you
uh for some, you know, in terms of being
able to, you know, recover them any max
draw down that you suffer in your
portfolio. You have time for the markets
to recover. Now, you know, if you said
that in 1968 or 71, you know, guess
what? You didn't recover for, you know,
a decade plus. So, just be aware of
that. Um, but, you know, you have time
for uh uh markets to recover. uh what
you uh so if you're a young investor but
if you're you know let's say I don't
know 50 55 60 65 someone at or near
retirement you don't necessarily have
time for your portfolio to recover from
a significant draw down of you know
let's say 20% or more of the the amount
of money you spent your entire career
accumulating again you go to work five
six sometimes seven days a week over the
course of multiple decades to save the
money that you save in your your your
your retirement account if you lose lose
20% of that, it's equivalent to losing
20% of all those work days that you just
basically pissed away because you are
too arrogant to to manage risk or you
you think you have you think the markets
are going to recover very quickly. One
of these crashes the market's not going
to recover very quickly. I don't know
which one it is. I I think we'll have a
very high probability uh forward-looking
scenario on that outcome whenever it
occurs. But who you I'm not going to I'm
not smart enough to say when the ne when
that eventually occurs. But guess what?

to do what they used to do, which is go
down for a long period of time and take
a long period of time to recover. Again,
everyone my age and below has never
experienced that in their investment
portfolio or in their investing career.

opportunity to experience, but I
guarantee you it will happen to you. And
these silly questions, and no, no
offense to the client, but these silly
questions about taxes and managing risk
and and and just, you know, will start
to have make a lot more sense. what I'm
saying right now in response to the

So just be aware of that. That's one
thing to consider. So uh kind of in
summary, uh taxes is not something you
can build an investment strategy around
uh in the context of having clients in

situations and and and their brokerage
account configurations or um you know,
in terms of being tax advantaged or not

investment strategy with that many
variables to account for that many
variables. And also, by the way, if
you're a young person, buy and hold
makes a lot more sense to you than if
you're someone who spent, you know, two,
three, four decades accumulating assets
so that you can have assets to to to
finance a comfortable retirement and
also leave your kids uh and your
community behind uh uh some wealth. So,
uh these are all things you have to

manages properly and avoids volatility
